questions.questionText:
The sum of squares of all the real solutions of the equation

$\log _{(x+1)}\left(2 x^2+5 x+3\right)=4-\log _{(2 x+3)}\left(x^2+2 x+1\right)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\log _{(x+1)}\left(2 x^2+5 x+3\right)=4-\log _{(2 x+3)}\left(x^2+2 x+1\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\log _{(x+1)}(x+1)(2 x+3)=4-\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\log _{(x+1)}(x+1)+\log _{(x+1)}(2 x+3)=4-2 \log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $1+\frac{1}{\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)}=4-2 \log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)$

Substitute $\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)=\boldsymbol{t}$

$$\therefore $$ $1+\frac{1}{\boldsymbol{t}}=4-2 \boldsymbol{t} \Rightarrow 2 \boldsymbol{t}^2-3 \boldsymbol{t}+1=0$

$\Rightarrow(\boldsymbol{t}-1)(2 \boldsymbol{t}-1)=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{t}=\left\{1, \frac{1}{2}\right\}$

For t = 1,

$\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow 2 x+3=x+1 \Rightarrow x=-2$

For t = $\frac{1}{2}$,

$\log _{(2 x+3)}(x+1)=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow(x+1)^2=2 x+3 \Rightarrow x^2=2 \Rightarrow x= \pm \sqrt{2}$

j xj x + 1 Base 2x + 3 Base 2x2 + 5x + 3 Argument x2 + 2x + 1 Argument Remarks Verdict
1 −2 −1 < 0 −1 < 0 1 > 0 1 > 0 1st and 2nd base −ve Invalid
2 −√2 1 − √2 < 0 3 − 2√2 > 0 7 − 5√2 < 0 3 − 2√2 > 0 1st base and 1st argument −ve Invalid
3 √2 1 + √2 > 0 3 + 2√2 > 0 7 + 5√2 > 0 3 + 2√2 > 0 All bases and arguments +ve Valid

$\sum\limits_{j=1}^3\left[x_j\right]^2=0+0+(\sqrt{2})^2=2$
questions.instruction: Enter the correct numerical value.

questions.questionText:
Let $\alpha=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{16}+\ldots \infty$ and

$\beta=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{27}+\ldots \infty$. Then the value of

$(0.2)^{\log _{\sqrt{5}}(\alpha)}+(0.04)^{\log _5(\beta)}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We first find the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

Given

$$ \alpha=\frac14+\frac18+\frac1{16}+\cdots $$

This is an infinite geometric progression with

$$ a=\frac14,\quad r=\frac12 $$

So,

$$ \alpha=\frac{a}{1-r}=\frac{\frac14}{1-\frac12}=\frac{\frac14}{\frac12}=\frac12 $$

Now,

$$ \beta=\frac13+\frac19+\frac1{27}+\cdots $$

This is also an infinite geometric progression with

$$ a=\frac13,\quad r=\frac13 $$

Thus,

$$ \beta=\frac{a}{1-r}=\frac{\frac13}{1-\frac13}=\frac{\frac13}{\frac23}=\frac12 $$

So both are equal to $\frac12$.

Now the expression becomes

$$ (0.2)^{\log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)}+(0.04)^{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)} $$

Convert decimals into fractions:

$$ 0.2=\frac15,\qquad 0.04=\frac1{25}=5^{-2} $$

So,

$$ \left(\frac15\right)^{\log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)}+\left(\frac1{25}\right)^{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)} $$

That is,

$$ 5^{-\log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)}+5^{-2\log_5\left(\frac12\right)} $$

Now use the change of base idea:

$$ \log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)=\frac{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)}{\log_5(\sqrt5)} $$

Since

$$ \log_5(\sqrt5)=\log_5\left(5^{1/2}\right)=\frac12 $$

therefore

$$ \log_{\sqrt5}\left(\frac12\right)=\frac{\log_5\left(\frac12\right)}{1/2}=2\log_5\left(\frac12\right) $$

Hence first term:

$$ 5^{-\log_{\sqrt5}(1/2)}=5^{-2\log_5(1/2)} $$

So both terms are same:

$$ 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}+5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}=2\cdot 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)} $$

Now use the identity

$$ a^{\log_a x}=x $$

Then

$$ 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}=5^{\log_5(1/2)^{-2}} \text{ is not correct} $$

So let us simplify properly:

$$ -2\log_5\left(\frac12\right)=\log_5\left(\frac12\right)^{-2}? $$

Instead, use

$$ k\log_a b=\log_a(b^k) $$

Thus,

$$ -2\log_5\left(\frac12\right)=\log_5\left(\left(\frac12\right)^{-2}\right)=\log_5(4) $$

Therefore,

$$ 5^{-2\log_5(1/2)}=5^{\log_5 4}=4 $$

So the total value is

$$ 2\times 4=8 $$

Hence, the required value is

$$ \boxed{8} $$

So the correct option is Option C.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The sum of all the real solutions of the equation $\log _{(x+3)}\left(6 x^2+28 x+30\right)=5-2 \log _{(6 x+10)}\left(x^2+6 x+9\right)$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \log _{(x+3)}\left(6 x^2+28 x+30\right)=5-2 \log _{(6 x+10)}\left(x^2+6 x+9\right)-1 \\ & \log _{(x+3)} 2\left(3 x^2+14 x+15\right)+2 \log _{(6 x+10)}\left(x^2+6 x+9\right)=5 \\ & \log _{(x+3)} 2(x+3)(3 x+5)+2 \log _{(6 x+10)}(x+3)^2=5 \\ & \log _{(x+3)}(x+3)+\log _{(x+3)}(6 x+10)+4 \log _{(6 x+10)}(x+3)=5 \\ & \log _{(x+3)}(6 x+10)+4 \log _{(6 x+10)}(x+3)=4 \quad\left\{\log _{(x+3)}(x+3)=1\right\}\end{aligned}$

Let, $\log _{(x+3)}(6 x+10)=t$

$\Rightarrow \log _{(6 x+10)}(x+3)=\frac{1}{t}$

$$\therefore $$ $t+4 \times \frac{1}{t}=4$

$\begin{aligned} & t^2-4 t+4=0 \\ & (t-2)^2=0 \Longrightarrow t=2\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Checking } x=1 \text { in equation (1) } \\ & \log _{(1+3)}\left(6(1)^2+28(1)+30\right)=5-2 \log _{(6(1)+10)}\left(1^2+6(1)+9\right) \\ & \log _4 64=5-2 \log _{16} 16 \\ & 3=5-2=3 \text { true } \\ & \text { Checking } x=-1 \text { in equation (1) } \\ & \log _{(-1+3)}\left(6(-1)^2+28(-1)+30\right)=5-2 \log _{(6(-1)+10)}\left((-1)^2+6(-1)+9\right) \\ & \log _2 8=5-2 \log _4 4 \\ & 3=3 \text { true } \\ & \text { So, solutions are } x=1 \text { and } x=-1 \\ & \text { Sum of solution }=1+(-1)=0\end{aligned}$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The product of all solutions of the equation $\mathrm{e}^{5\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right)^2+3}=x^8, x>0$, is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We begin with the equation:

$ e^{5(\log_e x)^2 + 3} = x^8, \; x > 0. $

Equating the exponents, we have:

$ 5(\log_e x)^2 + 3 = \log_e x^8 = 8 \log_e x. $

Let $ t = \log_e x $. Substituting, the equation becomes:

$ 5t^2 + 3 = 8t. $

Rewriting this as a quadratic equation:

$ 5t^2 - 8t + 3 = 0. $

Factoring the quadratic:

$ 5t^2 - 5t - 3t + 3 = 0, $

$ (5t - 3)(t - 1) = 0. $

Thus, the solutions for $ t $ are:

$ t = 1 $ implies $ \log_e x = 1 $, giving $ x = e $.

$ t = \frac{3}{5} $ implies $ \log_e x = \frac{3}{5} $, giving $ x = e^{\frac{3}{5}} $.

The product of all solutions is:

$ e^1 \times e^{\frac{3}{5}} = e^{1 + \frac{3}{5}} = e^{\frac{8}{5}}. $
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The number of integral solutions $$x$$ of $$\log _{\left(x+\frac{7}{2}\right)}\left(\frac{x-7}{2 x-3}\right)^{2} \geq 0$$ is :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\log _{\left(x+\frac{7}{2}\right)}\left(\frac{x-7}{2 x-3}\right)^{2} \geq 0$$

Domain :

$$ \begin{aligned} & x+\frac{7}{2}>0 \\\\ & x>\frac{-7}{2} \\\\ & x+\frac{7}{2} \neq 1 \\\\ & x \neq \frac{-5}{2} \\\\ & \frac{x-7}{2 x-3} \neq 0 \\\\ & x \neq 7 \\\\ & x \neq \frac{3}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Taking intersection : } x \in\left(\frac{-7}{2}, \infty\right)-\left\{-\frac{5}{2}, \frac{3}{2}, 7\right\} $$

Now $\log _{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{b} \geq 0$ if $\mathrm{a}>1$ and $\mathrm{b} \geq 1$

$$ \begin{gathered} \text { Or } \\\\ a \in(0,1) \text { and } b \in(0,1) \end{gathered} $$

Case I : $$ x+\frac{7}{2}>1 \text { and }\left(\frac{x-7}{2 x-3}\right)^2 \geq 1 $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$ x > -\frac{5}{2} $$

and

$$ \begin{aligned} & (2 x-3)^2-(x-7)^2 \leq 0 \\\\ & (2 x-3+x-7)(2 x-3-x+7) \leq 0 \\\\ & (3 x-10)(x+4) \leq 0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ x \in\left[-4, \frac{10}{3}\right] $$

$$ \text { Intersection : } \mathrm{x} \in\left(\frac{-5}{2}, \frac{10}{3}\right] $$

Case II : $$ x+\frac{7}{2} \in(0,1) \text { and }\left(\frac{x-7}{2 x-3}\right)^2 \in(0,1) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore 0 < x+\frac{7}{2}<1 \\\\ & -\frac{7}{2} < x < \frac{-5}{2} \end{aligned} $$

and

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{x-7}{2 x-3}\right)^2<1 \\\\ & \Rightarrow (x-7)^2 < (2 x-3)^2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$ x \in(-\infty,-4) \cup\left(\frac{10}{3}, \infty\right) $$

No common values of $\mathrm{x}$.

Hence intersection with feasible region.

We get $x \in\left(\frac{-5}{2}, \frac{10}{3}\right]-\left\{\frac{3}{2}\right\}$

Integral value of $x$ are $\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3\}$

No. of integral values $=6$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Let a, b, c be three distinct positive real numbers such that $${(2a)^{{{\log }_e}a}} = {(bc)^{{{\log }_e}b}}$$ and $${b^{{{\log }_e}2}} = {a^{{{\log }_e}c}}$$.

Then, 6a + 5bc is equal to ___________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given, $(2 a)^{\ln a}=(b c)^{\ln b}$, where $2 a>0, b c>0$

$$ \Rightarrow \ln a(\ln 2+\ln a)=\ln b(\ln b+\ln c) $$ ..........(i)

and $(b)^{\ln 2}=(a)^{\ln c}$

$$ \Rightarrow \ln 2 \cdot \ln b=\ln c \cdot \ln a $$ ..........(ii)

Now, let $\ln a=x, \ln b=y$

$$ \ln 2=p, \ln c=z $$

Now, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$ p \cdot y=x z \text { and } x(p+x)=y(y+z) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore p=\frac{x z}{y} \\\\ & \therefore x\left(\frac{x z}{y}+x\right)=y(y+z) \\\\ & \Rightarrow x^2 z+x^2 y=y^2(y+z) \\\\ & \Rightarrow \left(x^2-y^2\right)(y+z)=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \therefore $$ $ x^2 = y^2 $

$ x = \pm y $

So, from the equations, there are two cases :

Case 1 :

$ x = y $

In this case, since x and y are natural logarithms of positive numbers a and b respectively, this implies that a = b. However, this cannot be true as a, b, and c are given to be distinct positive real numbers.

Case 2 :

x = -y

In this case, $ \ln a = -\ln b $

$ \Rightarrow a \times b = 1$

$ \Rightarrow b = \frac{1}{a} $

Also, y + z = 0 (from the equations above)

$ \Rightarrow \ln b + \ln c = 0 $

$ \Rightarrow \ln (b \times c) = 0 $

$ \Rightarrow b \times c = 1 $

Given $ b = \frac{1}{a} $ and $ b \times c = 1 $ $ \Rightarrow c = a $

Thus, in the case where x = -y, the possible values are :

$b = \frac{1}{a} $

$c = a$


$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { If } b c=1 \Rightarrow(2 a)^{\ln a}=1 \\\\ & \Rightarrow a=1 / 2 \\\\ & \text { So, } 6 a+5 b c=6\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+5=3+5=8 \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Enter the correct numerical value.

questions.questionText: If the solution of the equation $$\log _{\cos x} \cot x+4 \log _{\sin x} \tan x=1, x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$, is $$\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha+\sqrt{\beta}}{2}\right)$$, where $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ are integers, then $$\alpha+\beta$$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$${\log _{\cos x}}\cot x + 4{\log _{\sin x}}\tan x = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _{\cos x}}\cot x - 4{\log _{\sin x}}\cot x = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - {\log _{\cos x}}\sin x - 4 - 4{\log _{\sin x}}\cos x = 1$$

Let $${\log _{\cos x}}\sin x = t$$

$$t + {4 \over t} = 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow t = 2$$

$$\sin x = {\cos ^2}x$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = 1 - {\sin ^2}x$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}x + \sin {x^{ - 1}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{ - 1\, \pm \,\sqrt 5 } \over 2}$$

as $$x \in \left( {0,{\pi \over 2}} \right)$$

$$\sin x = {{\sqrt 5 - 1} \over 2}$$

$$x = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{ - 1 + \sqrt 5 } \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = - 1,\beta = 5$$

$$\alpha + \beta = 4$$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $$S = \left\{ {\alpha :{{\log }_2}({9^{2\alpha - 4}} + 13) - {{\log }_2}\left( {{5 \over 2}.\,{3^{2\alpha - 4}} + 1} \right) = 2} \right\}$$. Then the maximum value of $$\beta$$ for which the equation $${x^2} - 2{\left( {\sum\limits_{\alpha \in s} \alpha } \right)^2}x + \sum\limits_{\alpha \in s} {{{(\alpha + 1)}^2}\beta = 0} $$ has real roots, is ____________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log _2\left(9^{2 \alpha-4}+13\right)-\log _2\left(\frac{5}{2} \cdot 3^{2 \alpha-4}+1\right)=2 \\\\
& \Rightarrow \frac{9^{2 \alpha-4}+13}{\frac{5}{2} 3^{2 \alpha-4}+1}=4 \\\\
& \Rightarrow \alpha=2 \quad \text { or } \quad 3 \\\\
& \sum_{\alpha \in \mathrm{S}} \alpha=5 \text { and } \sum_{\alpha \in \mathrm{S}}(\alpha+1)^2=25 \\\\
& \Rightarrow x^2-50 x+25 \beta=0 \text { has real roots } \\\\
& \Rightarrow \beta \leq 25 \\\\
& \Rightarrow \beta_{\max }=25
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.instruction: Enter the correct numerical value.

The number of solutions of the equation

$${\log _{(x + 1)}}(2{x^2} + 7x + 5) + {\log _{(2x + 5)}}{(x + 1)^2} - 4 = 0$$, x > 0, is :
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.questionText: The number of solutions of the equation log<sub>4</sub>(x $$-$$ 1) = log<sub>2</sub>(x $$-$$ 3) is _________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$${\log _4}(x - 1) = {\log _2}(x - 3)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2}{\log _2}(x - 1) = {\log _2}(x - 3)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _2}{(x - 1)^{1/2}} = {\log _2}(x - 3)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(x - 1)^{1/2}} = {\log _2}(x - 3)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(x - 1)^{1/2}} = x - 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow x - 1 = {x^2} + 9 - 6x$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 7x + 10 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - 2)(x - 5) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 2,5$$

But x $$ \ne $$ 2 because it is not satisfying the domain of given equation i.e. log2(x $$-$$ 3) $$ \to $$ its domain x > 3

finally x is 5

$$ \therefore $$ No. of solutions = 1.
questions.instruction: Enter the correct numerical value.

questions.questionText:
The number of distinct solutions of the equation
$${\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left| {\sin x} \right| = 2 - {\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left| {\cos x} \right|$$ in the interval [0, 2$$\pi $$], is ____.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$${\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left| {\sin x} \right| = 2 - {\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left| {\cos x} \right|$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left| {\sin x} \right|$$ + $${\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left| {\cos x} \right|$$ = 2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\log _{{1 \over 2}}}\left( {\left| {\sin x\cos x} \right|} \right)$$ = 2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\left| {\sin x\cos x} \right| = {1 \over 4}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ sin 2x = $$ \pm $$ $${1 \over 2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Number of distinct solution = 8
questions.instruction: Enter the correct numerical value.

questions.questionText:
For the following two (02) items:
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta $ be the roots of the quadratic equation
$(x^{2}+(\log _{0.5}(\alpha ^{2}))x+(\log _{0.5}(\alpha ^{2}))^{4}=0$
where $a ^ 2 \ne1$ and $\log _{0.5}(\alpha ^{2})>0$ Further, $\beta ^{2}=\alpha (\log _{\alpha ^{2}}(0.5))$
 
What is ẞ equal to?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let

$$k=\log_{0.5}(\alpha^2)$$

Then the given quadratic equation becomes

$$x^2+kx+k^4=0$$

and its roots are $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

So, by Vieta’s formulas,

$$\alpha+\beta=-k \qquad \text{and} \qquad \alpha\beta=k^4$$

Also given:

$$\beta^2=\alpha\left(\log_{\alpha^2}(0.5)\right)$$

Now use the change of base relation:

$$\log_{\alpha^2}(0.5)=\frac{1}{\log_{0.5}(\alpha^2)}=\frac{1}{k}$$

So the condition becomes

$$\beta^2=\frac{\alpha}{k}$$

Hence,

$$\alpha=k\beta^2$$

Now substitute this into the product relation:

$$\alpha\beta=k^4$$

So,

$$(k\beta^2)\beta=k^4$$

$$k\beta^3=k^4$$

Since $k>0$, divide by $k$:

$$\beta^3=k^3$$

Thus,

$$\beta=k$$

because $k>0$.

But $k=\log_{0.5}(\alpha^2)$, therefore

$$\beta=\log_{0.5}(\alpha^2)$$

So the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\log_{0.5}(\alpha^2)}$$

That is, Option 2.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $\log _b a=p, \log _d c=2 p$ and $\log _f e=3 p$, then what is $(a c e)^{\frac{1}{p}}$ equal to ?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To determine what $(a c e)^{\frac{1}{p}}$ equals, let's first make use of the given values and the properties of logarithms.

Given:

$$\log_b a = p$$

$$\log_d c = 2p$$

$$\log_f e = 3p$$

We can express $a$, $c$, and $e$ in terms of $p$:

From the first equation, $\log_b a = p$, we get:

$$b^p = a$$

From the second equation, $\log_d c = 2p$, we get:

$$d^{2p} = c$$

From the third equation, $\log_f e = 3p$, we get:

$$f^{3p} = e$$

Now, we need to find $(a c e)^{\frac{1}{p}}$:

Substituting the values we found:

$$a = b^p$$

$$c = d^{2p}$$

$$e = f^{3p}$$

Hence,

$$a c e = b^p \cdot d^{2p} \cdot f^{3p}$$

Now we raise this product to the power $\frac{1}{p}$:

$$\left(b^p \cdot d^{2p} \cdot f^{3p}\right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$$

Using the property of exponents $(x^a)^b = x^{ab}$, we get:

$$b^{p \cdot \frac{1}{p}} \cdot d^{2p \cdot \frac{1}{p}} \cdot f^{3p \cdot \frac{1}{p}}$$

Simplifying the exponents:

$$b^1 \cdot d^2 \cdot f^3$$

$$b \cdot d^2 \cdot f^3$$

Therefore, the expression $(a c e)^{\frac{1}{p}}$ simplifies to $b \cdot d^2 \cdot f^3$.

The correct option is:

Option A: $bd^2 f^3$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: What is the number of solutions of $\log_4(x - 1) = \log_2(x - 3)$?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To find the number of solutions to the equation $$\log_4(x - 1) = \log_2(x - 3)$$, we need to understand the relationship between the logarithmic bases.

We can use the change of base formula to rewrite the term on the left side in terms of base 2 logarithms. Recall that the change of base formula is:

$$\log_b(a) = \frac{\log_k(a)}{\log_k(b)}$$

Here, we want to rewrite $$\log_4(x - 1)$$ in terms of base 2. Since 4 is $$2^2$$, we can express it as:

$$\log_4(x - 1) = \frac{\log_2(x - 1)}{\log_2(4)}$$

Since $$\log_2(4) = \log_2(2^2) = 2$$, we have:

$$\log_4(x - 1) = \frac{\log_2(x - 1)}{2}$$

Now our equation becomes:

$$\frac{\log_2(x - 1)}{2} = \log_2(x - 3)$$

To eliminate the fraction, we multiply both sides of the equation by 2:

$$\log_2(x - 1) = 2 \log_2(x - 3)$$

Using the power rule of logarithms, which states $$a \log_b(c) = \log_b(c^a)$$, we can rewrite the right side of the equation:

$$\log_2(x - 1) = \log_2((x - 3)^2)$$

Since the bases are the same, we can set the arguments equal to each other:

$$x - 1 = (x - 3)^2$$

Now, we solve the resulting quadratic equation. Expand the square on the right side:

$$x - 1 = x^2 - 6x + 9$$

Rearrange all terms to one side of the equation to set it to zero:

$$x^2 - 6x + 9 - x + 1 = 0$$

Simplify the equation:

$$x^2 - 7x + 10 = 0$$

Now we solve this quadratic equation either by factoring, completing the square, or using the quadratic formula. This quadratic can be factored as:

$$(x - 2)(x - 5) = 0$$

Setting each factor equal to zero gives us the potential solutions:

$$x - 2 = 0 \implies x = 2$$

$$x - 5 = 0 \implies x = 5$$

However, these solutions must be checked to ensure they satisfy the original logarithmic equations. Both values must make the arguments of the logarithms positive:

For $$x = 2$$:

$$x - 1 = 1$$ (which is positive)

$$x - 3 = -1$$ (which is not positive; hence, $$x = 2$$ is not a valid solution)

For $$x = 5$$:

$$x - 1 = 4$$ (which is positive)

$$x - 3 = 2$$ (which is positive; hence, $$x = 5$$ is a valid solution)

Therefore, there is only one valid solution:

Option B: One
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: For $x \geq y > 1$, let $\log_x\left( \frac{x}{y} \right) + \log_y\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = k$, then the value of $k$ can never be equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We can simplify the given expression using the change of base formula and properties of logarithms:

$$ \begin{aligned} \log_x\left( \frac{x}{y} \right) + \log_y\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) &= \frac{\log\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)}{\log x} + \frac{\log\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}{\log y}\\\\ &= \frac{\log x - \log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log y - \log x}{\log y}\\\\ &= 1 - \frac{\log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log y}{\log y} - \frac{\log x}{\log y}\\\\ &= 1 + \frac{\log y}{\log y} - \left(\frac{\log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log x}{\log y}\right)\\\\ &= 2 - \left(\frac{\log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log x}{\log y}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Now, let's analyze the expression $$ \frac{\log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log x}{\log y} $$. Since $$x \ge y > 1$$, we know that $$ \log x \ge \log y > 0$$. Therefore, both $$ \frac{\log y}{\log x}$$ and $$ \frac{\log x}{\log y}$$ are positive. By AM-GM,

$$ \frac{\log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log x}{\log y} \ge 2\sqrt{\frac{\log y}{\log x} \cdot \frac{\log x}{\log y}} \ge 2. $$

This means $$ 2 - \left(\frac{\log y}{\log x} + \frac{\log x}{\log y}\right) \le 0$$. In other words, $$k \le 0$$. Therefore, $$k$$ can never be equal to $$\boxed{1}$$.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: What is the minimum value of the function f(x) = log10(x2 + 2x + 11) ?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To find the minimum value of the function $$f(x) = \log_{10}(x^2 + 2x + 11)$$, we first need to analyze the expression inside the logarithm: $$g(x) = x^2 + 2x + 11$$.

This is a quadratic function in the form $$ax^2 + bx + c$$, where $$a = 1$$, $$b = 2$$, and $$c = 11$$. The quadratic function opens upwards because the coefficient of $$x^2$$ is positive. Therefore, the minimum value of $$g(x)$$ occurs at the vertex of the parabola.

The x-coordinate of the vertex for a quadratic function $$ax^2 + bx + c$$ is given by:

$$x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

Substituting the values of $$a$$ and $$b$$, we get:

$$x = -\frac{2}{2 \cdot 1} = -1$$

Now we substitute $$x = -1$$ back into the quadratic function $$g(x)$$ to find the minimum value:

$$g(-1) = (-1)^2 + 2(-1) + 11 = 1 - 2 + 11 = 10$$

Thus, the minimum value of $$x^2 + 2x + 11$$ is 10. Now, we need to find the logarithm of this minimum value:

$$f(-1) = \log_{10}(10)$$

Since $$\log_{10}(10) = 1$$, the minimum value of the function $$f(x)$$ is:

$$f(x) = 1$$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: 1
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If f(x) = ln (x + $\sqrt{1+\text{x}^2}$), then which one of the following is correct ?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To find the correct option, we first need to analyze the function given:

$$f(x) = \ln \left( x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} \right)$$

We are asked to evaluate the function's behavior with respect to $ x $ and $ -x $. Let's define $ f(-x) $ and see how it relates to $ f(x) $.

Substitute $ -x $ into the function:

$$f(-x) = \ln \left( -x + \sqrt{1 + (-x)^2} \right)$$

Simplify the expression inside the logarithm:

$$f(-x) = \ln \left( -x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} \right)$$

Now, let's add $ f(x) $ and $ f(-x) $:

$$ f(x) + f(-x) = \ln \left( x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} \right) + \ln \left( -x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} \right) $$

Using the property of logarithms, $ \ln(a) + \ln(b) = \ln(ab) $, we get:

$$ f(x) + f(-x) = \ln \left( \left( x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} \right) \left( -x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} \right) \right) $$

Evaluate the product inside the logarithm:

$$ ( x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} ) ( -x + \sqrt{1 + x^2} ) = -x^2 + (1 + x^2) = 1 $$

So, we have:

$$ f(x) + f(-x) = \ln(1) $$

We know that $ \ln(1) = 0 $. Therefore:

$$ f(x) + f(-x) = 0 $$

Hence, the correct option is:

Option A: $ f(x) + f(-x) = 0 $
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: A radioactive substance decays at a rate proportional to the amount of substance present. If half of the substance decays in 100 years, then what is the decay constant (proportionality constant) ?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To determine the decay constant for a radioactive substance that decays at a rate proportional to the amount present, we can use the formula for exponential decay:

$$N(t) = N_0 e^{-kt}$$

where:

  • $$N(t)$$ is the amount of substance at time $$t$$
  • $$N_0$$ is the initial amount of the substance
  • $$k$$ is the decay constant (proportionality constant)
  • $$t$$ is the time

Given that the half-life of the substance (the time it takes for half of the substance to decay) is 100 years, we can set $$N(t) = \frac{N_0}{2}$$ and $$t = 100$$.

Using these values in the exponential decay formula, we get:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-k \cdot 100}$$

Dividing both sides of the equation by $$N_0$$ gives:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-100k}$$

Taking the natural logarithm (ln) of both sides, we get:

$$\ln \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = \ln \left(e^{-100k}\right)$$

Since $$\ln \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$$ simplifies to $$-\ln(2)$$ and $$\ln \left(e^{-100k}\right)$$ simplifies to $$-100k$$, we can write:

$$-\ln(2) = -100k$$

Solving for $$k$$, we get:

$$k = \frac{\ln(2)}{100}$$

Therefore, the decay constant for the radioactive substance is:

Option A: $$\frac{\ln 2}{100}$$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If log10 2 log2 10 + log10(10x) = 2, then what is the value of x?
options: log52
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let's break down the equation step by step:

The given equation is:

$$\log_{10} 2 \cdot \log_2 10 + \log_{10}(10^x) = 2.$$

Notice that the logarithm of an exponent simplifies as follows:

$$\log_{10}(10^x) = x.$$

So, the equation becomes:

$$\log_{10} 2 \cdot \log_2 10 + x = 2.$$

A key logarithmic identity is:

$$\log_a b \cdot \log_b a = 1.$$

Here, with $ a = 10 $ and $ b = 2 $, we have:

$$\log_{10} 2 \cdot \log_2 10 = 1.$$

Substitute this value back into the equation:

$$1 + x = 2.$$

Solve for $ x $:

$$x = 2 - 1 = 1.$$

Thus, the value of $ x $ is 1, which corresponds to Option B.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The value of x, satisfying the equation  $log_{cos x} ~sin x = 1$, where $0<x<\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The given equation is

$$ \log_{\cos x}(\sin x) = 1. $$

This implies:

$$ \sin x = (\cos x)^1 = \cos x. $$

Thus, we have:

$$ \sin x = \cos x. $$

By dividing both sides by $\cos x$, we get:

$$ \tan x = 1. $$

The solutions to $\tan x = 1$ are given by:

$$ x = \frac{\pi}{4} + k\pi $$

where $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. However, the constraint is $0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$. Therefore, the solution that satisfies this condition is:

$$ x = \frac{\pi}{4}. $$

Therefore, the value of $x$ satisfying the equation is:

Option C: $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
If n = 100!, then what is the value of the following?

$\rm \dfrac{1}{log_2n}+\dfrac{1}{log_3n}+\dfrac{1}{log_4n}+{.....}+\dfrac{1}{log_{100}n}$
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Concept

Product Rule: The logarithm of a product is equal to the sum of the logarithms of the individual factors.

$ \log_a(mn) = \log_a m + \log_a n $

Change of Base Rule: This relates logarithms of different bases to each other.

$ \log_m n = \frac{1}{\log_n m} $

Calculation

Given the expression:

$ \frac{1}{\log_2 n} + \frac{1}{\log_3 n} + \frac{1}{\log_4 n} + \ldots + \frac{1}{\log_{100} n} $

Using the change of base rule, this can be simplified to:

$ \log_n 2 + \log_n 3 + \log_n 4 + \ldots + \log_n 100 $

This sum is equivalent to:

$ \log_n (2 \times 3 \times 4 \times \ldots \times 100) $

Which simplifies to:

$ \log_n (100!) $

Since $ n = 100! $, the expression becomes:

$ \log_{100!} (100!) $

This evaluates to:

$ 1 $

Thus, the value of the given expression is 1.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $${x^{{{\log }_7}x}} > 7$$ where x > 0, then which one of the following is correct?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$${x^{{{\log }_7}x}} > 7$$ where x > 0.

Taking log on base 7 both sides

$${\log _7}x.{\log _7}x > {\log _7}7$$ [$$\because$$ $${\log _a}{m^n} = n{\log _a}m$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {({\log _7}x)^2} > 1$$ [$$\because$$ $${\log _a}a = 1$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _7}x > ( \pm \,1)$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x > {7^1} \Rightarrow x > 7$$

and $$x > {7^{ - 1}} \Rightarrow x < {1 \over 7}$$

Hence, $$x \in \left( {{1 \over 7},7} \right)$$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If 0 < a < 1, the value of log10 a is negative. This is justified by
options: Negative power of 10 is less than 1
Negative power of 10 is between 0 and 1
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let log10 a = x

$$\Rightarrow$$ a = 10x

It is given that 0 < a < 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ 0 < 10x < 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ x must be negative.

$$\therefore$$ If 0 < a < 1, the value of log10 a is negative implies that negative power of 10 is between 0 and 1.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: If $$x + {\log _{15}}(1 + {3^x}) = x{\log _{15}}5 + {\log _{15}}12$$, where x is an integer, then what is x equal to?
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have,

$$x + {\log _{15}}(1 + {3^x}) = x{\log _{15}}5 + {\log _{15}}12$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _{15}}{15^x} + {\log _{15}}(1 + {3^x}) = lo{g_{15}}{5^x} + {\log _{15}}12$$

[$$\because$$ $${\log _a}a = 1$$ and $${\log _b}{a^m} = m{\log _b}a$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _{15}}[{15^x}(1 + {3^x})] = lo{g_{15}}({5^x} \times 12)$$ [$$\because$$ $$\log a + \log b = \log ab$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {15^x}(1 + {3^x}) = 12\,.\,{5^x}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3^x}(1 + {3^x}) = 12$$

$$ \Rightarrow y(1 + y) = 12$$ [where, $$y = {3^x}$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} + y - 12 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (y + 4)(y - 3) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = - 4,3$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3^x} = - 4,3$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3^x} = 3$$ [$$\because$$ $${3^x} \ne - 4$$]

$$\therefore$$ x = 1
